ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 covers milk tea as a category. No specific entry exists for wildflower honey oolong tea latte; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages. No specific code exists for honey oolong milk tea latte; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 74242007 represents tea as a substance. No specific concept exists for honey oolong milk tea latte; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large cup (approximately 500 ml / 500 g) as served at KOI Thé, representing a standard large-size Taiwanese milk tea serving.

nutrient_source.title: Milk tea, prepared with whole milk and sugar — estimated composition from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for brewed oolong tea, whole milk, and honey, combined in proportions consistent with a standard Taiwanese milk tea latte formulation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for wildflower honey oolong tea latte. Values estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for brewed oolong tea (FDC ID 1104262), whole milk (FDC ID 746782), and honey (FDC ID 169640) in approximate proportions of 50% brewed tea, 45% whole milk, and 5% honey by weight per 100 g of beverage. This is consistent with standard Taiwanese milk tea latte formulations. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of honey and milk-based beverages — analogous estimate based on honey GI (55, University of Sydney GI Database) and whole milk GI (27, University of Sydney GI Database), weighted by approximate proportions in formulation; oolong tea contributes negligible GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for wildflower honey oolong tea latte. GI estimated as weighted average: honey GI ~55 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008) at ~5% weight contribution and whole milk GI ~27 (University of Sydney GI Database) at ~45% weight contribution, with brewed oolong tea contributing negligible glycemic load. Weighted estimate yields approximately GI 45, categorised as low. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 51.0) / 100 ≈ 23.0, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 51.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~110 mg per large serving)
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 22 mg sodium per 100 g (110 mg per 500 ml serving), this beverage poses a low sodium burden and is generally acceptable for individuals managing hypertension, though the added honey contributes free sugars that should be accounted for in overall dietary intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content from honey and moderate glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The wildflower honey contributes approximately 9.8 g of free sugars per 100 g (approximately 49 g per large 500 ml serving) with an estimated glycemic load of 23 per serving, which is moderate-to-high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size or request reduced-honey variants to manage postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and low sodium; moderate sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage contains modest saturated fat (~0.9 g per 100 g) and low sodium, presenting a low cardiovascular risk profile per serving, though the free sugar content from honey warrants moderation in the context of a heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wildflower Honey Oolong Tea Latte (Large) is a Taiwanese-style milk tea beverage sold at KOI Thé outlets in Singapore, including the Funan location. It is prepared by brewing oolong tea and blending it with fresh whole milk and wildflower honey, resulting in a smooth, lightly floral, and subtly sweet drink with a creamy texture. The oolong tea base provides a characteristic amber colour and a mellow, slightly roasted flavour that is softened by the milk and complemented by the delicate floral sweetness of wildflower honey. As a large (approximately 500 ml) serving, it delivers roughly 310 kcal, 8 g of protein, 7.5 g of fat, and approximately 51 g of carbohydrates, predominantly from the honey and milk. The beverage contains milk allergens and is not suitable for vegans or those with lactose intolerance. Clinically, the high free sugar content from honey (~49 g per large serving) makes this a moderate-to-high glycemic load beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, who should consider reduced-honey options. The low sodium content makes it acceptable for hypertensive individuals in moderation. Oolong tea polyphenols may offer antioxidant benefits, though these are not quantified in standard nutrient databases. This drink is popular in Singapore's bubble tea and specialty tea café culture and is representative of the Taiwanese tea latte category.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for wildflower honey oolong tea latte. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for brewed oolong tea, whole milk, and honey in proportions consistent with standard Taiwanese milk tea latte formulations. GI is an analogous estimate derived from University of Sydney GI Database values for honey and whole milk. Medium confidence is appropriate given the estimation methodology.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (brewed oolong tea FDC ID 1104262, whole milk FDC ID 746782, honey FDC ID 169640) in approximate proportions of 50% brewed tea, 45% whole milk, 5% honey by weight. Serving size of 500 g is an approximation for a large Taiwanese milk tea cup. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for these ingredients; naturally zero in unprocessed dairy and honey at these proportions). dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 (naturally zero in liquid tea, milk, and honey beverage with no solid plant matter). vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources at this blended formulation level. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy entries used for estimation. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. GI set to 45 (low category) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for honey (GI ~55) and whole milk (GI ~27), weighted by approximate proportions; oolong tea contributes negligible glycemic load. GL per large 500 g serving = (45 × 51.0) / 100 ≈ 23.0. Food is served at KOI Thé - Funan, Singapore. Price SGD 7.70. This record requires re-verification if KOI Thé publishes official nutrition data.

Documented zero values for dietary_fibre_g and trans_fat_g with justification phrases in notes.
Documented -1 sentinel values for vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg in notes.
Set GI to 45 (numeric > 0, low category) using analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database.
Calculated GL per 500 g serving as 23.0.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
